food_name: Mili Juli Sabzi (Spicy Mixed Vegetable Curry)
food_subgroup: Cooked mixed vegetable dish with spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Mili Juli Sabzi; FOODON_03311737 (mixed vegetable dish) is the closest defensible match for a cooked spiced mixed vegetable preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Mixed vegetables, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for Indian spiced mixed vegetable curry in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional Indian vegetable preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mili Juli Sabzi is approximately 200 g, served as a side dish or main vegetable accompaniment with roti or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetable Curry (Sabzi) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, composite mixed vegetable curry entry; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central mixed cooked vegetables reference for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exactly matches Mili Juli Sabzi (spicy). Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) composite entry for mixed vegetable curry (sabzi), which covers seasonal vegetables cooked with oil, onion, tomato, and spices. Macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked mixed vegetables (FDC ID 170072) and adjusted upward for oil and spice content typical of a restaurant preparation. Energy estimated at approximately 85 kcal/100 g reflecting moderate oil use in a restaurant context.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed cooked vegetables and vegetable-based Indian dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mixed vegetables and vegetable curries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mili Juli Sabzi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) cooked mixed vegetables typically GI 30–45 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) the presence of tomatoes, onions, and green chillies which are low-GI ingredients; (3) the spice-and-oil cooking method which does not significantly raise GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 18.4) / 100 = 7.4, using 200 g serving with 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Restaurant-prepared Mili Juli Sabzi may contain 300–400 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and high dietary fibre content supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of approximately 40 and 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Mili Juli Sabzi is a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a standard 200 g serving yields a glycaemic load of approximately 7.4, which is within the low GL range and unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile, high vegetable fibre, and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mili Juli Sabzi contains negligible saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), no cholesterol, and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from cooking oil, making it a heart-friendly vegetable dish; however, sodium content should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mili Juli Sabzi (Spicy Mixed Vegetable Curry) is a classic North Indian vegetable preparation in which a medley of seasonal fresh vegetables — commonly including cauliflower, peas, carrots, beans, potatoes, and capsicum — are sautéed or stir-fried with freshly ground spices, tomatoes, onions, and green chillies. The name 'Mili Juli' (Hindi: मिली जुली) literally means 'mixed together', and 'Sabzi' (सब्ज़ी) means 'vegetables', making this a dish defined by its variety and seasonal flexibility. In restaurant contexts in Singapore, it is typically served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or rice, and is a staple on North Indian restaurant menus. The spicy variant uses a more generous quantity of green chillies and freshly pounded masala, giving the dish a pronounced heat and aromatic depth. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 85 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g), and is rich in vitamins A and C from the vegetable base. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load per serving (~7.4) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content may be elevated in restaurant preparations due to added salt, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a nutritionally dense, plant-forward option that supports vegetable intake goals in a South Asian dietary pattern.
common_user_queries: Is Mili Juli Sabzi good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Mili Juli Sabzi (spicy) exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest ICMR-NIN composite mixed vegetable curry entry and cross-referenced with USDA cooked mixed vegetable data, with upward adjustment for restaurant oil and salt use. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mixed vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-characterised and the ingredient profile is consistent across sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no single approved-source entry exactly matches Mili Juli Sabzi (spicy) as served in Singapore. Values reflect a typical restaurant preparation with moderate oil use. 2. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA reference entries used for this adaptation. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in vegan foods. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as plant-based vegetable dishes contain negligible vitamin D; this is naturally zero for this food type. 5. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 6. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~40) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mixed vegetables; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 8. Sodium content of 320 mg/100 g is a restaurant-context estimate; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. 9. The food_id GD000095 is from the field survey data seed dataset and is not present in the FD series food_index.json.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry composite entry with USDA cross-check. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries. Added all mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Indian cuisine in Singapore context. Documented all zero and -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. All hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria satisfied in first iteration.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per validator feedback, aligning with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] and the documented gap notes.
